subsystem: SoundTouch BPMDetect — the beats-per-minute (tempo) detection class of the SoundTouch open-source audio time-stretch/pitch library. This is the constructor BPMDetect::BPMDetect(int numChannels, int aSampleRate), which is handed the channel count and sample rate of an audio stream and sets up the decimation factor, autocorrelation window sizes, and the working buffers (xcorr, beatcorr ring buffer, FIFO sample buffer, Hamming windows) used to estimate BPM from decoded PCM.
provenance: Identified by the class/method name BPMDetect::BPMDetect and the member/constant vocabulary that is unique to SoundTouch's BPMDetect.cpp: beat_lpf(_LPF_coeffs), decimateBy/decimateSum/decimateCount, windowLen/windowStart, xcorr, beatcorr_ringbuff, FIFOSampleBuffer, hamming()/hamw/hamw2, and the tuning macros TARGET_SRATE, MIN_BPM, MAX_BPM_RANGE, INPUT_BLOCK_SIZE, DECIMATED_BLOCK_SIZE, XCORR_UPDATE_SEQUENCE. The sample banner comment `/* sample: primevul_validation_0229_vuln */` and the filename token cwe617 corroborate a reachable-assertion focus.
trust_boundary: The two constructor parameters numChannels and aSampleRate are the untrusted inputs. In real deployments BPMDetect is fed by a decoder: sampleRate and channel count come straight from an audio container/codec header (WAV fmt chunk, MP3/AAC frame header, Ogg, etc.), which an attacker fully controls by crafting a media file. Nothing in this constructor validates that aSampleRate is a sane, in-range audio rate before it is used as a divisor and as an allocation-size driver. TARGET_SRATE in SoundTouch is 1000, so any sampleRate below 1000 (including 0, 1, or a negative value) is hostile input to this code.

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the BPMDetect constructor. It takes two int parameters: numChannels (the number of audio channels in the source stream) and aSampleRate (the source sample rate in Hz). The member-initializer list constructs beat_lpf, a low-pass IIR filter object, from the fixed coefficient table _LPF_coeffs. Both parameters are attacker-influenced because they normally originate from a decoded media header. Note both are declared int (signed), which matters for the arithmetic and allocation that follow.

beats is a std::vector that accumulates detected beat positions; reserve(250) pre-allocates capacity to avoid repeated reallocation during processing (a fixed, benign constant). The constructor then copies the raw, unvalidated parameters into the member fields this->sampleRate and this->channels. From this point on, the class uses sampleRate everywhere as trusted, but it was never range-checked — the attacker-controlled value is now baked into the object state.

Initializes the decimation accumulator (decimateSum) and its counter (decimateCount) to zero. During processing SoundTouch averages every decimateBy input samples down to one decimated sample to reach roughly a 1 kHz internal rate; these two fields hold the running sum and count of samples not yet flushed. Pure initialization, no external input consumed here.

decimateBy is computed as integer division sampleRate / TARGET_SRATE, i.e. how many input samples map to one ~1 kHz decimated sample (TARGET_SRATE is 1000 in SoundTouch). The code then asserts decimateBy > 0, and asserts that INPUT_BLOCK_SIZE is smaller than decimateBy * DECIMATED_BLOCK_SIZE. These asserts encode the implicit precondition the author knew was required — that the sample rate is large enough for the decimation math to be well-formed. They are the only sanity checks on sampleRate, and they are asserts, not runtime validation: they abort in asserts-enabled builds and vanish entirely under NDEBUG.

These lines derive the autocorrelation window bounds from the tempo search range. windowLen corresponds to the slowest tempo (MIN_BPM) and windowStart to the fastest (MAX_BPM_RANGE); both are computed as (60 * sampleRate) divided by (decimateBy * a BPM constant), converting 'beats per minute' into a lag length in decimated samples. windowLen becomes the length of the two big heap arrays allocated below. A third assert requires windowLen > windowStart. Note decimateBy appears in both denominators, so if it is zero these are divisions by zero; and 60 * sampleRate is a signed int multiply that can overflow for large sampleRate.

Allocates the cross-correlation accumulator array xcorr with windowLen float elements using new[], then zero-fills it with memset over windowLen * sizeof(float) bytes. windowLen is the value computed at line 19 from the untrusted sample rate, so both the element count passed to new[] and the byte count passed to memset are attacker-influenced. new float[windowLen] converts windowLen to size_t; a negative windowLen becomes an enormous size_t.

Initializes correlation bookkeeping (pos, peakPos, peakVal, init_scaler, beatcorr_ringbuffpos) and then allocates a second heap array, beatcorr_ringbuff, again sized windowLen floats and zeroed with memset over windowLen * sizeof(float) bytes. This ring buffer stores beat-correlation history. It shares the exact same windowLen-driven sizing risk as xcorr, so any bad windowLen affects two allocations.

Allocates a FIFOSampleBuffer (SoundTouch's growable sample queue) to hold incoming samples, forces it to mono (setChannels(1)) because BPM detection collapses to a single channel, and clears it. numChannels was stored earlier but the actual processing buffer is fixed to 1 channel here, so numChannels is not used to size anything in this constructor.

Allocates two Hamming-window coefficient arrays: hamw of length XCORR_UPDATE_SEQUENCE and hamw2 of length XCORR_UPDATE_SEQUENCE/2, and fills each by calling hamming(). XCORR_UPDATE_SEQUENCE is a compile-time constant, so these two allocations and window computations are fully deterministic and independent of the untrusted sample rate — the only genuinely safe allocations in the constructor.

End of the constructor. On successful completion the object holds two windowLen-sized heap arrays, two Hamming windows, a mono FIFO buffer, and the stored sampleRate/channels. Every heap array is owned raw (new[]) and must be released with delete[] in the destructor; this snippet does not show that cleanup, and it also does not show any exception-safety handling if a later new[] throws after an earlier one succeeded.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: sampleRate is attacker-controlled and never validated. TARGET_SRATE is 1000, so any sampleRate in the range 0..999 makes the integer division decimateBy = sampleRate / TARGET_SRATE evaluate to 0. The guard against that is assert(decimateBy > 0) — a reachable assertion (CWE-617). In any build compiled with assertions enabled, a crafted audio file declaring a sub-1000 Hz sample rate causes assert() to call abort(), crashing the whole host application: a remotely triggerable denial of service through nothing more than a malformed header. The implementation must instead validate aSampleRate with a real runtime check (reject rates that make decimateBy < 1) and return/throw gracefully rather than relying on assert.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
windowLen = (60 * sampleRate) / (decimateBy * MIN_BPM);
    windowStart = (60 * sampleRate) / (decimateBy * MAX_BPM_RANGE);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the same root cause as above but with a worse impact in production. Release builds define NDEBUG, which makes assert(decimateBy > 0) at line 15 expand to nothing. With the guard compiled out, decimateBy == 0 flows straight into these two expressions, and the denominators decimateBy * MIN_BPM and decimateBy * MAX_BPM_RANGE both become 0. Integer division by zero is undefined behavior — on x86 it raises SIGFPE and terminates the process. So the very builds that ship to users (asserts off) convert the 'assertion DoS' into a hard divide-by-zero crash (CWE-369). The invariant: decimateBy must be proven non-zero by a real check before it is ever used as a divisor.

windowLen is a signed int derived from 60 * sampleRate. sampleRate is a signed int the attacker controls, so for a large declared rate (roughly sampleRate > 35.7 million) the product 60 * sampleRate overflows INT_MAX, which is undefined behavior and can yield a negative or wrapped windowLen (CWE-190). A negative windowLen passed to new float[windowLen] is converted to size_t, producing either a std::bad_array_new_length exception or an absurd allocation request; and memset(xcorr, 0, windowLen * sizeof(float)) then computes a size_t byte count from that same bad value. If windowLen is small-but-wrong (e.g. reduced by wraparound) the arrays are undersized while later processing indexes them by lag up to the intended window, giving out-of-bounds writes. The implementation must clamp/validate sampleRate to a realistic audio range so windowLen stays a sane positive size before allocating.

A second reachable assertion (CWE-617). Because decimateBy is attacker-influenced (it shrinks as sampleRate shrinks), a low-but-nonzero sampleRate that yields a small decimateBy can make decimateBy * DECIMATED_BLOCK_SIZE fail to exceed INPUT_BLOCK_SIZE, tripping this assert and aborting in asserts-enabled builds. Under NDEBUG the assert disappears and the block-size relationship the author assumed no longer holds, which can desynchronize the decimation/block loop in the implementation. Same fix: enforce the precondition with real validation, not assert.

A third reachable assertion (CWE-617). windowLen and windowStart are both attacker-influenced through sampleRate and the integer divisions above; degenerate inputs (very small sampleRate, or overflow) can make windowLen <= windowStart, tripping abort in asserts-enabled builds. Under NDEBUG the invariant windowLen > windowStart is simply assumed by downstream correlation code, so a violated relationship can drive loop bounds and index math into out-of-range territory. This assert is documentation of a required invariant that is never actually enforced at runtime.

Secondary robustness issue: the constructor acquires several raw new[]/new resources (xcorr at 25, beatcorr_ringbuff at 33, FIFOSampleBuffer at 37, hamw/hamw2 at 43/45) with no try/catch. If any later allocation throws std::bad_alloc — very plausible given windowLen is attacker-sized and could be enormous — the already-allocated earlier buffers leak because the destructor does not run for a constructor that throws (CWE-401). Even in the normal path, these raw new[] arrays must be matched with delete[] (not delete, not free) in the destructor to avoid CWE-762 mismatched deallocation. Prefer std::vector<float>/std::unique_ptr<float[]> so sizing failures and cleanup are handled safely.

summary: The BPMDetect constructor trusts its aSampleRate parameter — which in practice comes from an attacker-controlled media header — and uses it as a divisor and as a heap allocation size without any validation. A sample rate below TARGET_SRATE (1000 Hz) drives decimateBy to 0, tripping the reachable assertion assert(decimateBy > 0) (CWE-617, DoS) in debug builds and, once NDEBUG strips that assert, a hard integer division-by-zero (CWE-369) in shipped release builds; large sample rates additionally overflow the signed windowLen computation feeding new float[windowLen]. The fix is real range validation of aSampleRate before any division or allocation, not asserts.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: BPMDetect::BPMDetect(int numChannels, int aSampleRate) — constructor signature
reviewed_anchors.reason: aSampleRate is untrusted and unvalidated; it is the source of the reachable-assertion / divide-by-zero / overflow chain.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: beat_lpf(_LPF_coeffs) — member init from fixed coefficient table (line 3)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Constructed from a compile-time constant table, no attacker input.
Fixed constant capacity reservation; no security contract.
Stores the untrusted rate verbatim with no range check; propagates to all downstream math.
Stored but the processing buffer is forced to mono (line 39); numChannels is not used to size any buffer in this constructor.
Plain zero-initialization of accumulators.
Integer division yields 0 for sampleRate < TARGET_SRATE(=1000); root of the reachable-assertion and divide-by-zero chain.
Reachable assertion (CWE-617) on attacker-controlled value; aborts in debug, vanishes under NDEBUG leaving a divide-by-zero.
Reachable assertion (CWE-617) on decimateBy-derived expression; also silently disabled under NDEBUG.
Divide-by-zero when decimateBy==0; signed overflow of 60*sampleRate; drives allocation size.
Same decimateBy==0 divide-by-zero and 60*sampleRate overflow hazard.
Reachable assertion (CWE-617) enforcing an invariant that is not checked at runtime; violated by degenerate/overflowed inputs.
Allocation sized by attacker-influenced windowLen; negative/overflowed value → bad_array_new_length or under/over-sized buffer; raw new[] with no exception safety.
Byte count derived from the same untrusted windowLen; covered by the overflow/allocation finding.
Constant initialization of correlation bookkeeping fields.
Second windowLen-sized raw allocation with the identical sizing and cleanup contract.
Same windowLen byte-count risk already captured by the overflow/allocation finding.
Fixed mono buffer; not sized by untrusted input. Raw new is part of the exception-safety/cleanup finding.
Sized by a compile-time constant; deterministic and safe (cleanup only).
Compile-time-constant size; safe (cleanup only).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: TARGET_SRATE / MIN_BPM / MAX_BPM_RANGE / INPUT_BLOCK_SIZE / DECIMATED_BLOCK_SIZE / XCORR_UPDATE_SEQUENCE macros
reviewed_anchors.reason: Compile-time tuning constants; their only security relevance is as divisor/threshold operands, captured in the divide-by-zero/assertion findings.

findings.title: Reachable assertion on attacker-controlled sample rate (decimateBy == 0)
findings.bug_class: Reachable assertion / denial of service (unvalidated input)
findings.invariant: aSampleRate must be validated (rejected with a runtime error, not an assert) so that sampleRate / TARGET_SRATE >= 1 before decimateBy is used; assert() must never be the guard for externally-controllable input.
findings.failure_mode: An audio file declaring a sample rate below TARGET_SRATE (1000 Hz) — e.g. 0, 1, or 999 — makes the integer division yield decimateBy == 0. assert(decimateBy > 0) then calls abort(), terminating the host application. This is a remotely triggerable DoS from a malformed media header in any assertions-enabled build.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no runtime range check on aSampleRate exists before line 14, and that assert is the only barrier; confirm how BPMDetect is instantiated from decoded stream metadata.

findings.title: Integer division by zero in release (NDEBUG) builds
findings.code:
windowLen = (60 * sampleRate) / (decimateBy * MIN_BPM);
    windowStart = (60 * sampleRate) / (decimateBy * MAX_BPM_RANGE);
findings.bug_class: Division by zero (assert compiled out under NDEBUG)
findings.invariant: decimateBy must be proven non-zero by a real runtime check (not assert) before it is used as a divisor at lines 19-20.
findings.failure_mode: Release builds define NDEBUG, so assert(decimateBy > 0) at line 15 expands to nothing. With decimateBy == 0 (any sampleRate < 1000), both denominators decimateBy * MIN_BPM and decimateBy * MAX_BPM_RANGE are 0, causing an integer divide-by-zero: SIGFPE / undefined behavior that crashes the shipped process. The 'DoS via assert' becomes a hard crash precisely in production builds.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the project builds with NDEBUG for release and that no other guard precedes lines 19-20; reproduce with sampleRate=0 in a release build.

findings.title: Signed integer overflow sizing heap allocations (windowLen)
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow leading to bad allocation size / OOB
findings.invariant: aSampleRate must be clamped to a realistic audio range so that 60 * sampleRate does not overflow int and windowLen remains a sane positive count before it sizes new float[windowLen] and the memset byte count.
findings.failure_mode: sampleRate is a signed int from an untrusted header. For sampleRate greater than about 35.79 million, 60 * sampleRate overflows INT_MAX (undefined behavior), producing a negative or wrapped windowLen. new float[windowLen] converts it to size_t → std::bad_array_new_length or an absurd allocation; a wrap to a small-but-wrong windowLen yields under-sized xcorr/beatcorr_ringbuff arrays that later correlation code indexes out of bounds (CWE-787/125). memset then uses the same tainted size.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the declared type of windowLen and sampleRate (int) and that no upper bound on aSampleRate is enforced; test with a very large sampleRate to observe the allocation/allocation-failure behavior.

findings.title: Additional reachable assertions on decimateBy/windowLen invariants
findings.invariant: The block-size relationship (INPUT_BLOCK_SIZE < decimateBy * DECIMATED_BLOCK_SIZE) and the window ordering (windowLen > windowStart) must be enforced by real validation of aSampleRate, since both derive from the untrusted rate.
findings.failure_mode: Degenerate sample rates (small decimateBy, or overflow-corrupted windowLen/windowStart) can make either assertion false, aborting in debug builds. Under NDEBUG both asserts vanish and the assumed invariants are consumed by downstream decimation/correlation loops, which can drive block and index math out of range. These asserts document preconditions that are never actually enforced at runtime.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm downstream loops in BPMDetect processing rely on these invariants and behave incorrectly (or OOB) when they are violated with asserts disabled.

findings.title: Raw new[]/new resources with no exception safety or shown delete[]
findings.anchor: xcorr = new float[windowLen]; beatcorr_ringbuff = new float[windowLen]; buffer = new FIFOSampleBuffer();
findings.bug_class: Resource leak on constructor throw / mismatched deallocation risk
findings.invariant: Each raw new[]/new must be released exactly once with the matching delete[]/delete in the destructor, and allocations acquired in the constructor must be exception-safe so a later throw does not leak earlier buffers.
findings.failure_mode: windowLen is attacker-sized, so a large value can make new float[windowLen] throw std::bad_alloc after xcorr already succeeded; because the destructor does not run for a constructor that throws, the earlier buffers (and FIFOSampleBuffer) leak (CWE-401). Separately, all four arrays are float[] via new[] and must be freed with delete[] (not delete/free) to avoid CWE-762 mismatched deallocation. RAII (std::vector/std::unique_ptr) would remove both hazards.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the destructor uses delete[] for xcorr/beatcorr_ringbuff/hamw/hamw2 and delete for buffer, and that partial-construction failure paths do not leak.

audit_checklist: Add a runtime range check on aSampleRate (reject rates that make sampleRate/TARGET_SRATE == 0 and cap an upper bound) BEFORE line 14, returning/throwing gracefully instead of relying on assert.
Confirm decimateBy != 0 before it is used as a divisor at lines 19-20 to eliminate the NDEBUG divide-by-zero (SIGFPE).
Verify how BPMDetect is instantiated: trace whether sampleRate flows directly from a decoded/attacker-controlled media header.
Check the declared types of sampleRate and windowLen (int) and bound 60 * sampleRate against INT_MAX to prevent overflow feeding new float[windowLen].
Ensure the two asserts at lines 16 and 22 are backed by real validation, since they are compiled out under NDEBUG.
Confirm the destructor releases xcorr, beatcorr_ringbuff, hamw, hamw2 with delete[] and buffer with delete, and make constructor allocations exception-safe (prefer std::vector/std::unique_ptr).
Reproduce with sampleRate = 0 and sampleRate = 999 in both a debug (abort) and release (SIGFPE) build to validate the fix.
